coredata.dc:title: Effects of elemental doping, acid treatment, and passivation on the fluorescence intensity and emission behavior of yellow fluorescence carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Shaofeng
Wang, Muqun
Gao, Wei
Zhao, Xia
coredata.dc:description: Hydrothermal methods are commonly used to synthesize carbon dots (CDs), but the synthesized CDs have the disadvantage of low quantum yields. In this paper, we used acid treatment, elemental doping, and passivation to treat the CDs, which could increase the quantum yield from 36% to 42%, 61% and 68%, respectively, and the doping and passivation treatments resulted in red-shifted and blue-shifted emission behavior of the CDs. The optical properties, morphology and chemical structure of CDs are analyzed to investigate the reasons for the quantum yield increase and the mechanism of excitation dependent emission (EDE) and excitation independent emission (EIE). The increase of quantum yield is mainly due to the increase of pyridine N content. The different optical behaviors depend mainly on the surface states of CDs. C–OH and C–O–C can create new energy levels leading to the appearance of EDE properties. C=O is an electron-absorbing group that weakens the conjugation effect leading to blue shift.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Optical behavior
Quantum yield
Fluorescence mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Emission spectra of CDs under various excitation wavelengths. (a) CDs-1 (b) CDs-2 (c) CDs-3 (d) CDs-4.

TEM of (a) CDs-1 (scale:20 nm) (b) CDs-2 (scale:20 nm) (c) CDs-3 (scale:50 nm) (d) CDs-4 (scale:50 nm); HRTEM of (e) CDs-1 (scale:2 nm) (f) CDs-2 (scale:2 nm) (g) CDs-3 (scale:5 nm) (h) CDs-4 (scale:50 nm); Size distribution of (i) CDs-1 (j) CDs-2 (k) CDs-3 (l) CDs-4 (m) DLS of CDs-4.
(a) FTIR spectra (4000–500 cm−1) of CDs-1, -2, -3, -4 (b) FTIR spectra (2250–500 cm−1) of CDs-1, -2, -3, -4.
XPS survey spectra (a) CDs-1 (e) CDs-2 (i) CDs-3 (m) CDs-4 and high-resolution XPS of the (b,f,j,n) C1s, (c,g,k,o) N1s, and (d,h,l,p) O1s spectra.
Schematic diagram of the effect of emission behavior.
content of different bonds in C1s for four CDs.
content of different bonds in N1s for four CDs.
content of different bonds in O1s for four CDs.

head.ce:title: Effects of elemental doping, acid treatment, and passivation on the fluorescence intensity and emission behavior of yellow fluorescence carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Engineering and Technology Research Center for High Quality Structural Panels from Biomass Wastes, Nanning, Guangxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Engineering and Technology Research Center for High Quality Structural Panels from Biomass Wastes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Engineering and Technology Research Center for High Quality Structural Panels from Biomass Wastes, Nanning, Guangxi, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal methods are commonly used to synthesize carbon dots (CDs), but the synthesized CDs have the disadvantage of low quantum yields. In this paper, we used acid treatment, elemental doping, and passivation to treat the CDs, which could increase the quantum yield from 36% to 42%, 61% and 68%, respectively, and the doping and passivation treatments resulted in red-shifted and blue-shifted emission behavior of the CDs. The optical properties, morphology and chemical structure of CDs are analyzed to investigate the reasons for the quantum yield increase and the mechanism of excitation dependent emission (EDE) and excitation independent emission (EIE). The increase of quantum yield is mainly due to the increase of pyridine N content. The different optical behaviors depend mainly on the surface states of CDs. C–OH and C–O–C can create new energy levels leading to the appearance of EDE properties. C=O is an electron-absorbing group that weakens the conjugation effect leading to blue shift.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging carbon nanomaterials with unique properties [1]. Compared with traditional fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have the advantages of small size, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and photostability [2–4]. This has led to a wide range of applications in photocatalysis [5–7], sensors [8–11], bioimaging [10,12], and light-emitting devices [13–15]. The presence of many reactive groups on the surface of CDs provides the possibility of functionalization and further broadens the application scope of CDs [16–18]. However, CDs have the shortcomings of low quantum yield and strong emission only in the blue region, which greatly limit the application scope of CDs. Currently, atomic doping, surface modification and other methods are used to improve and tune the inherent optical properties of CDs to expand their potential applications.

To enhance the fluorescence performance of CDs, atomic doping is by far the most common method. Broadly speaking, the doped atoms can be classified into metallic and nonmetallic atoms. Currently, metallic elements such as Cu [19,20], Fe [21], Zn [22] and more. Rare earth elements such as lanthanum [23], gadolinium [24], terbium [25] have been reported. The doping of some metal atoms induces a narrowing of the band gap in the energy level of CDs and changes the energy levels of the ground and excited states of CDs, thus enhancing the fluorescence performance [26]. Among the non-metal atoms, nitrogen [21,27,28] doping is the most common, in addition to sulfur [29], boron [30], phosphorus [31], selenium [32], silicon [33], and even halogen elements: fluorine [34],chlorine [32,35]. Nitrogen doping increases the electron cloud density of CDs, modulates the electron energy level, increases the probability of radiative leap, and thus increases their quantum yields [36,37]. Differences in the type and form of doped atoms can lead to differences in the modulation effect and mechanism of fluorescence properties.

The formation of CDs assisted by acid also enhances fluorescence performance. H2SO4 has a better effect than HCl, HNO3, and HClO4 [38]. H2SO4 is not volatile and remains acidic during the reaction, and the H2SO4-assisted system can avoid the formation of large particles and thus promote the formation of high-quality CDs [39]. As CDs are increasingly studied, the surface passivation of CDs is also effective in tuning the optical properties of CDs. Among the passivation treatments, polyethylene glycol (PEG) is commonly used. The fluorescence intensity of CDs modified with PEG is enhanced [40], while remaining stable in solution [41]. PEG passivation treatment can also convert CDs from non-fluorescent to fluorescent or even multicolor fluorescence [41–43].

In this paper, we used the conventional hydrothermal method to synthesize yellow fluorescent CDs and adopted three different methods to treat CDs and compare them with untreated CDs (blank group). The morphology and chemical structure of CDs were analyzed in detail, and it was found that the chemical structure of CDs is the root cause of the changes in quantum yield and optical behavior. Nitrogen-related functional groups in CDs can effectively regulate the quantum yield. Oxygen-related functional groups can regulate the energy level and change the excitation independent emission (EIE) of CDs. In this paper, a detailed analysis of CDs synthesized by the hydrothermal method is presented, which can guide the synthesis of CDs with desirable optical properties using the hydrothermal method.

O-phenylenediamine (98%), urea (99%), thiourea (99%), polyethylene glycol (Mn400) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd., and sulfuric acid was purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. All water used in the experiment was deionized water.

CDs-1: 0.3g of o-phenylenediamine and 0.2g of urea were weighed, added to 40 mL of deionized water, fully dissolved and transferred to an autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The reactor was removed and cooled to room temperature, centrifuged at 4500 r/min for 10 min, and the precipitated material was removed to obtain a yellow-brown liquid. The obtained solution was added to a dialysis bag (MD: 3500) and dialyzed for 48 h. CDs-2: 0.3 g of o-phenylenediamine with 0.2 g of urea was added to 40 mL DI water, and 200 μL of H2SO4 was added after full dissolution, and the reaction process was the same as that of CDs-1. The obtained liquid was washed with ethanol to neutrality, heated to evaporate the excess ethanol, and dialysis was performed. CDs-3: CDs-3: Took 20 mL of purified CDs-1, added 0.2 g of thiourea, stirred evenly, and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. The solution was dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis bag (MD: 3500). CDs-4: 20 mL of purified CDs-1 was taken, 2 g of PEG-400 was added, stirred for 30 min, and reacted at 180 °C for 1 h. The resulting solution was extracted 3 times with ethyl acetate to remove unreacted reagents and then dialyzed for 48 h (Dialysis bag: MD: 7000) (Fig. 1 ).

TEM was performed by a transmission electron microscope (FEI TECNAI G2 F30, FEI); electron transition was performed by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent 8453, Agilent); Functional group testing was performed by FTIR (Nicolet iS 50, Thermo Fisher); Raman spectroscopy was performed by a Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR Evolution, HORIBA); elemental analysis was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (ESCALAB 250XI+, Thermo Fisher); fluorescence spectroscopy was performed by fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4, HORIBA); dynamic light scattering analysis was performed by a dynamic Light Scattering (LSI-3DLS, LS Instruments). Particle size was performed by nano measurer.

A commonly used method for measuring quantum efficiency, i.e., the reference method, was used. In the experiments of this chapter, rhodamine 6G was selected, dissolved in ethanol and prepared into a solution. CDs and rhodamine 6G were configured to the same concentration,the absorbance As and fluorescence spectrum of rhodamine 6G at the excitation peak of CDs was measured. Calculate the fluorescence integral area Is of the emission peak. Similarly, measuring the CDs absorbance Ax and fluorescence integral area Ix, and calculating the quantum yield of CDs according to the following formula:
1 φX = φS × Ix/Is × As/Ax × [ηX]2/[ηS]2

ηx is the refractive index of the solvent used in the unknown sample (the refractive index of water is 1.33), and ηS is the dissolved refractive index of the reference sample (the refractive index of ethanol is 1.36).

Graphene quantum dots synthesized by top-bottom route, accompanied by quantum confinement effects and edge effects [54]. However, the CDs synthesized by hydrothermal method (bottom-up),which morphological structure and chemical structure are the main reasons that affect fluorescence performance [55]. Therefore, the four CDs exhibit different emission behaviors, which may be caused by differences in the morphology and surface chemical structure of the CDs.

Firstly, the morphology of these CDs was observed, as shown in Fig. 3ãd. All nanoparticles show quasi-spherical shape. Compared with CDs-1, CDs-2 exhibit a more regular shape, which is mainly attributed to the assistance of H2SO4, which can accelerate the carbonization process while promoting the uniform nucleation and growth of carbon nanodots [56]. However, CDs-4, which was passivated by PEG, showed a larger spherical shape of the body. Further observation of CDs, HRTEM images are shown in Fig. 3e∼h. Lattices were observed in CDs-1, -2, -3, and the lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, which indicated that these three CDs possessed a graphitic-like structure. No lattice was observed for CDs-4. Fig. 3i∼l show the particle size distribution of the four nanoparticles, and it is clear that the particle size distribution of CDs-2 is narrower compared to the other CDs. The particle size distribution of CDs-4 ranges from 110 to 230 nm. A dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to detect CDs-4, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 m. The results of DLS show that there are no micron-sized particles in the CDs-4 solution, and the distribution range is narrow, which confirms that CDs-4 is a fluorescent particle that does not belong to the category of carbon quantum dots. However, the particle size distribution of CDs-3 is lower than 20 nm, but shows a different emission behavior from that of CDs-1 and -2. Most importantly, the optimal emission wavelengths of the four nanoparticles do not depend on the variation of the particle size distribution, which indicates that the size effect is not the reason for the different emission behaviors.

The Raman spectra of the four CDs are shown in Fig. 4 . CDs-1, -2, -3, and -4 show obvious graphitized D- and G-peak. The D-peak absorption bands, which represent amorphous carbon structures or defective structures in graphite-like materials, while the G-peak absorption bands represent regular or ordered structures of sp [2]-hybridized carbon atoms in graphite-like structures. D- and G-peak appeared at 1365 cm−1 and 1589 cm−1, indicating that the CDs have graphitic structural features. Compared with CDs-1, the G-peak absorption band of CDs-2 was stronger, which further confirmed that sulfuric acid promoted the carbonization of CDs. In addition, the G-peak absorption intensity of CDs-3 was enhanced, and the ratio of its G-peak to D-peak intensity was calculated as IG/ID = 1.37, and the increase of the ratio value indicates the enhancement of graphitization. We found that the peak intensity ratios of CDs-2, -4 are similar but exhibit inconsistent optical behavior, which further should confirm that the morphological structure of CDs is not the reason for the difference in emission behavior.

The fluorescence emission of CDs is closely related to the external surface state. To analyze the differences in emission behavior exhibited by CDs, we first analyzed the CDs using a UV spectrophotometer to probe the electron leap channels inside the CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 , it can be clearly seen that the peaks of the four CDs at 274–277 nm are relatively sharp, and all have the π-π* transition of the C=C bond [57]. In addition, all four CDs have n-π* jumps of C=O bonds at 350–370 nm. It is worth noting that comparing CDs-1 and CDs-3, CDs-1 shows an absorption band at 358 nm, while the absorption band of CDs-3 appears at 369 nm. The shift in the absorption band may be due to heteroatom doping [58]. The doping of heteroatoms introduces additional energy states into CDs, thereby changing the electronic transition path and increasing the fluorescence intensity [59].

Nitrogen and oxygen-related groups carried on the surface of CDs are important for their photoluminescent properties [28]. Therefore, we analyzed the functional groups of CDs. In Fig. 6 a, it can be seen that the absorption peaks located at 3446 cm−1 and 3202 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, respectively [60]. CDs-3 shows an absorption peak at 2072 cm−1 that is not present in other CDs, which can be attributed to the characteristic absorption peak of C≡C, indicating the presence of non-hexagonal structured carbon defects in CDs-3 [61]. The C=O stretching vibration of carboxylic acid appears at 1730 cm−1 and the absorption peak at 1628 cm−1 is generated by the bending vibration of –CONH– [27,62]. The absorption peak near 1485 cm−1 can be attributed to the skeleton vibration of the benzene ring. The four CDs synthesized are rich in nitrogen and oxygen related groups, which endow the CDs with good water solubility.

In order to observe the chemical bond composition of CDs in more detail, XPS was performed on the four CDs. All shown in Fig. 7 . The samples showed three representative peaks: C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), and O1s (532 eV), while CDs-3 showed S2p (169eV). The results show that C, N, O are present in the four CDs, and the element content is different. It is noteworthy that CDs-3, in which the S2p signal was detected, was doped with thiourea during the synthesis process. The nitrogen content of acid-treated CDs-2 was lower than that of CDs-1. We speculate that strong acid promotes the decomposition of o-phenylenediamine during heating. CDs-4 with a very low O content means that CDs-4 has fewer oxygen-containing groups, and the non-radiative recombination within CDs is then reduced, thus contributing to enhanced fluorescence performance. At the same time, CDs-4 has a high C content. We guess that PEG participates in the formation of carbon nuclei and will be carbonized to a certain extent at high temperatures. In C1s high-resolution spectra of the four CDs, the band is decomposed into four peaks, C=C/C–C (284 eV), C–O (286 eV), C=O (287 eV) and HO–C=O (289 eV). In the N1s high-resolution spectra, all appear at around 398.5, 399.8 for pyridinic N, pyrrolic N [63]. Nitrogen content of CDs-4 is the lowest, lacking C–NH2 at around 397.5 eV compared to the other three. Observing the O1s of CDs-3, -4, it has one more peak belonging to C–O–C than the other two CDs.

Further analysis of the behavior of groups containing nitrogen and oxygen related groups on the optical behavior was performed on the percentage content of different chemical bonds. Table .2 shows the percentage content of different chemical bonds in the four CDs. For CDs-3, -4 exhibit different optical behaviors from CDs-1, in terms of chemical bond content, both C–C/C=C and HO–C=O are less than CDs-1, while both C–O are more than CDs-1. The chemical bond that appears to be different is C=O. π -π* and n-π* leaps are jointly present in the structures of the four CDs. However, the n-π* transition is more likely to be the main electronic transition channel for electrons [64,65]. So we speculate that the C=O proportion is one of the reasons for the formation of the two EDE behaviors.

For the different nitrogen-related chemical bond contents (Table .3 ). Pyridinic N content of the three different treatments of CDs is higher than that of CDs-1. Liang et al. [66] have demonstrated that the presence of pyridine N can effectively improve the photoluminescence performance of CDs. Therefore, the rise in pyridinic N content is an important reason for the increase in PL intensity. Although pyridine N can effectively increase the fluorescence intensity, the fluorescence intensity increase effect brought by heteroatom doping is much stronger than that of pyridine N. Therefore, the pyridine N content of CDs-3 is lower than that of CDs-2, but the fluorescence intensity is higher than that of CDs-2. Unfortunately, although pyridine N can affect PL intensity, it is not the reason for the different emission behavior [67].

In CDs, oxygen-related bonds play an important role in creating energy gaps with adjustable PL [68]. The oxygen-related bonds are dominated by C–OH and C–O–C [69]. As shown in Table .4 , compared with CDs-1,-2, CDs-3,-4 have a large amount of C–OH and C–O–C, so they can create new energy gaps, get rid of the EIE properties, and exhibit EDE properties.

The EDE and EIE properties exhibited by CDs are explained (Fig. 8 ). For the EDE properties exhibited by CDs-3,-4, the presence of many oxygen-containing groups in both CDs results in a discrete distribution of the relevant energy levels in the n-π* electron leap channels [70]. The electron transitions from the ground state to the highest excited state and returns to the ground state, undergoing non-radiative relaxation. In this process, most of the electrons are captured by multiple energy levels and eventually return to the ground state, resulting in EDE. When the reduction of oxygen-containing groups and most nitrogen-containing groups are present, the relevant energy levels are then distributed more singularly in the n-π* electron leap channels, i.e., they exhibit EIE behavior. In response to the different EDE behaviors exhibited by CDs-3, -4, Ma et al. [71] clearly indicated that the increase of electron-absorbing groups would weaken the conjugation effect and thus cause blue-shift. From Table .3, it is known that the content of C=O in CDs-4 is significantly higher than that of CDs-3, which is a typical electron-absorbing group, thus causing the blue-shift.

In summary, we used a facile hydrothermal one-step method to synthesize yellow fluorescent CDs with o-phenylenediamine and urea as monomers, and enhanced the PL properties by, acidification, doping and passivation treatments. The doping and passivation treatments changed the EIE properties of the CDs and exhibited two distinct EDE properties: red-shift and blue-shift. The PL mechanism of the four CDs was analyzed and explored, and the nitrogen and oxygen related groups on the surface of CDs have an important relationship with EDE and EIE formation. Pyridinic N is important for improving PL performance, while C–OH and C–O–C can create new energy levels and change EIE properties. We combine our previous experience to suggest that C=O content is the root cause of red-shift and blue-shift.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
